chunk.text: 3 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Annual Precious Metal Report 17 January 2012 Overview At the start of 2011, we were bullish in our outlook for precious metals prices. However, we noted that slowing developed world economic activity and European debt concerns could prove problematic. The sell-off at the end of the year was therefore not entirely unexpected but it was deeper than we had previously thought likely, with prices weaken- ing more than expected, and only the gold price recording an annual gain. Many of the global concerns which dominated the news in 2011 will be important this year once again and gold might be expected to benefit most amongst the precious metals from the combination of sluggish growth and significant risk. Interest rates in the developed world are extremely unlikely to rise and economic growth is set to be weak (although the USA looks somewhat healthier than had been expected a few months ago). Quantitative easing in the USA looks less likely than before now that the economy there is showing some signs of better health.

We are unlikely to see large flows of capital from the bond market into commodities during this period. However, with yields already extraordinarily low (and Ger- man yields recently turning negative), only a sharp worsening of economic sentiment can be expected to drive further investment into bonds at the expense of the precious metals. In the equities markets, valuations are now low enough to make some shares attractive on a yield basis alone. The prospect of reasonable global economic growth suggests that some capital appreciation might be expected too, particularly where the relevant company is exposed to higher developing world growth. However, risk aversion and high price volatility are likely to make equity investment only for the brave in early 2012 unless equity prices make a determined move higher or price volatility decreases. If this happens, the prospect of equity outperformance could attract some capital from the commodities themselves into the shares of the mining companies.

However, prospects look better for higher metal prices later in the year as the risk of holding the Euro gradually decreases. Competition from other assets For an investor, the precious metal complex does not exist in a vacuum. To attract investment, and increase in value, these metals must either appear to have better prospects of price appreciation than other assets, or exhibit the benefits of asset diversification or of being a safe haven, or show some other attractive attribute. Currently, bond yields for a few favoured countries are extremely low as quantitative eas- ing has increased bond prices and investors have fled to those currencies perceived as safest. Although long term bond yields appear to be unattractive in general, the perception of these few bonds (American, British, German and Japanese in particular) as safe, even if they are not risk-free, means that they will remain favoured while the risk of European defaults remains.

chunk.text: 5 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Annual Precious Metal Report 17 January 2012 The Euro crisis The early December “fiscal compact” or mini-treaty agreed by almost all the member states of the European Union seems to be an attempt to address two separate issues. Firstly, politicians have belatedly realised that a currency union requires some fiscal responsibility. And, where this responsibility is not forthcoming voluntarily, it will have to be mandated. Secondly, the compact appears to be an attempt to reassure Germany in particular that the debtor nations will show a greater level of responsibility. The theory is that this partial loss of sovereignty of the debtor nations is the price to pay for German permission for the ECB to relax its lending rules in order to act as lender of last resort or to accede to proposals of issuing joint Eurobonds. The first aim, that of encouraging or mandating fiscal responsibility amongst the member states is an important longer term component of stabilising the Euro and some progress appears to have been made towards this.

chunk.text: ity amongst the member states is an important longer term component of stabilising the Euro and some progress appears to have been made towards this. Unfortunately, the second aim appears not to have been achieved with the rhetoric coming from the German Government and the ECB not having softened at all. As a result, the summit appears to have focused on the longer term problems without addressing the shorter term ones. This is rather like standing on a railway track with a train approaching and writing to your parliamentary representative to argue for better safety on the railways rather than jumping out of the way of the oncoming train: a reasonable action but not the one required at that time. Given that even the fiscal compact is not fully defined and that a number of countries will separately have to ratify any agreement, it still seems likely to us that not every one of the 26 countries which initially signaled their assent will sign a final deal. Depending on the number of those that do finally sign (and whether that includes all of the Eurozone countries) this summit may have achieved little other than alienating the UK.

chunk.text: ose that do finally sign (and whether that includes all of the Eurozone countries) this summit may have achieved little other than alienating the UK. However, there are more useful actions taking place within Europe. The ECB has repeatedly insisted that it would not buy sovereign bonds in significant volumes and would not embark upon quantitative easing. However, just before Christmas, it revealed that it had leant over €400 billion in three year funding to Eurozone banks. This should at least ease some of the liquidity problems that have been concerning the market. And, whether or not it qualifies under the technical definition of quantitative easing, it does look very similar to quantitative easing. It is not clear how much of this money will be lent into the real economy or used to buy further sovereign bonds. However, by solving the current liquidity question, it has improved the economic outlook. If this is close enough to “printing money”, then this could be positive for asset prices. However, the size of the funding required should at least cause some concern amongst investors that all is not well with the European economy.

In any case, the first quarter of 2012 will be an interesting time for the Eurozone. The ratings agencies placed a large number of European countries on negative watch in December and the recent downgrades will add to the economic stress. Additionally, Italy in particular, will hold very large bond auctions in January and February. It is hard to fore- cast currency movements in this environment but there is certainly substantial downside risk to the Euro against the Dollar in the early part of the year. Effects of a real Euro crisis Our base case scenario for 2012 assumes that no countries are forced from the Euro and that it survives as a viable currency. If this is the case, it will gradually regain some poise and slow its seven month fall against the US Dollar (although an exchange rate of as low as $1.20 seems entirely possible). However, it is important to consider the possibility that a country will fall – or be pushed – from the Euro. Superficially, the most likely candidate to leave the Euro is Greece.

chunk.text: 6 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Annual Precious Metal Report 17 January 2012 further bailouts and major fiscal changes, which may prove societally unacceptable, Greece will not be able to service its debts and it may therefore be forced to leave the Euro. This would be enormously disruptive to the European economy but should, of itself, be manageable due to the relatively small size of the Greek economy. However, this would place extra pressure on other countries within the Eurozone which are running budget deficits and have large borrowings. Bond yields would rise for Ireland, Italy, Spain and probably even France as the permanence of the Euro came into question. It is also possible that other countries could leave the Euro with the negotiation and ratification process for the proposed fiscal compact liable to cause considerable friction between countries. However, the fact that 26 out of 27 EU countries agreed to this outline deal in early December demonstrates the desire to hold the Euro together. This partial resignation of national sovereignty was unimaginable only a few months earlier.

chunk.text: ould be huge short term problems. Political constraints on economic action Next year will see a few important elections or other political upheavals. Unfortunately, with these approaching, politicians’ focus tends to move from mundane matters such as ensuring the survival of a currency or presenting widespread economic recession towards the more important question of retaining or gaining power. In the Euro zone, the room for political manoeuvre is limited by the forthcoming French presidential elections (which start in April 2012). With this approaching, there seems little prospect of concerted French attempts to move towards a balanced budget in the first few months of the year, since this would involve making unpopular decisions on cutting benefits. The prospect of German federal elections in 2013 makes a fully-fledged, German-funded bail-out of any debtor countries or the issuing of Eurobonds on that time- scale very unlikely. Both of these situations suggest that the Eurozone crisis will continue to develop for some time. Just as importantly for the global economy, the US Presidential elections will take place in November 2012.

Given that this has occurred, and was essentially agreed in only a single day, it seems that politicians may finally be willing to act in the Euro’s interests and we therefore expect the Euro to survive 2012. However, should any country leave the Euro, we expect this to lead to a reduction of at least one percentage point in European economic output. Much larger disruptions are possible too in various more depressing scenarios. More importantly, liquidity problems would require the sale of all sorts of assets. We believe that this would be negative for almost all asset prices and poses a low probability but potentially large downside risk to our forecasts. The long term effect of such a move might be positive for Europe’s economy but there would be huge short term problems. s will continue to develop for some time. Just as importantly for the global economy, the US Presidential elections will take place in November 2012. Interestingly, sentiment on both sides of the political divide seems to agree that the US needs to make a start on reducing its budget deficit. So, after the elections, whoever wins, we could see a move towards shoring up of the USA’s finances. Unfortunately, before that point, the antagonistic and dysfunctional current nature of American party politics makes it unlikely that significant legislation will be passed. Although the US economy is growing acceptably well at the moment, there are fiscal headwinds approaching next year from the potential expiry of payroll tax cuts and unemployment insurance benefits. These have been temporarily extended and both parties may yet be able to find agreement on extending these measures further. However, if they do not, US economic growth could sink to a lethargic 1-2 per cent this year. The third major political issue is the election of the new Chinese Politburo which will take place in Autumn 2012. The Communist Party’s main pre-occupation leading up to this will be to ensure social stability.

We expect to see growth of between eight and nine per cent during 2012 as a result, with relatively little downside risk. Looking at the platinum group metals specifically, all of the major producing nations face leadership elections during the coming year. The National Conference of South Africa’s ANC will elect a leader in December 2012. It is not yet clear who the likely winner (and presumably the next leader of South Africa itself) will be. However, given the populist discussion over mine nationalisation, there could be upwards pressure on platinum prices ahead of this vote, regardless of the eventual winner and any policies they may adopt. Likewise, Zimbabwe faces an election at some point in 2012. Although current Government policy towards the mining industry seems rather confused, it seems clear that Zanu PF at least favours higher levels of local tax and local ownership of mining operations within Zimbabwe. With an election approaching, this populist, nationalist rhetoric is likely to intensify.

However, longer term ambition and expectations for higher prices can be badly affected by short term price performance. We therefore find three issues of greatest concern (in terms of price) for the first quarter of 2012. Firstly, the Euro crisis shows little sign of being solved one way or another in the first few months of 2012 if ever. As a result, concerns over the Euro could allow the Dollar to strengthen, placing downwards pressure on commodity prices. We do not expect this trend to last throughout the year but this could drag on commodity prices in the first and second quarters. The impact of the recent set of credit rating downgrades will be a critical test of market sentiment. 8 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Annual Precious Metal Report 17 January 2012 Liquidity issues and fear of recession could also have a significant impact. Investors may choose to further reduce positions held in many assets in the short term to reduce coun- terparty exposure while market stress exists, just as they did in the first stages of the global economic crisis, applying further downward pressure to metals prices. Finally, at the start of last year, we felt that the market had adopted a tone of irrational exuberance that the world economy had overcome most of its problems. This proved to be unfounded although it did allow the more industrial of the precious metals – palladium and platinum – to outperform gold. As reality dawned that Europe was digging itself into a fiscal hole, gold outperformed the other metals as a form of “safe haven”. We feel that an outbreak of unfettered optimism in Q1 2012 is unlikely despite the normal “January effect” where prices often rise at the start of the year. Instead we feel that exces- sive pessimism is the flavour of the day. With this in mind, we feel that gold has the poten- tial to outperform the other precious metals in Q1.

Price seasonality Over the last decade, there has been a reasonable degree of price seasonality for the ma- jor precious metals. Typically, prices have performed best during the first and last quarters of a year, on average. The extremely weak price performance of the four main precious metals in Q4 2011 should therefore worry investors in case it is a signal of bearish senti- ment developing within this sector. In fact, for most of the precious metals (especially gold, palladium and platinum), we feel that sentiment remains positive for a range of reasons, with expectations of higher prices (although recent meetings have given us a rather more negative prognosis for the silver price in the short term).

chunk.text: 9 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Annual Precious Metal Report 17 January 2012 Gold Forecast 2012 High: $2,050, Average: $1,755, Low: $1,470 Sentiment on gold has recently turned significantly more bearish (or at least less bullish). In part this is due to the challenges of believing that gold can extend its decade long run of annual gains and, in part, to the fall in prices during late 2011. However, many of the underlying driving forces for higher gold prices from 2011 will persist. Extreme economic uncertainty, low real interest rates, high developing world inflation and the prospect of European sovereign debt defaults will accompany us throughout 2012. We are, though, concerned that some risk fatigue seems to be developing in the gold market with ever greater market stresses needed to drive fresh buying interest. Signs of price sensitivity in the Indian physical market are also negative.

As the realisation sinks in that – in the absence of a Euro meltdown – the world economy will grow next year, the other metals may be able to gain some ground on gold. E8 versus G8 demand Many of the driving forces for higher gold prices have been widely attributed to Western or developed world economic activity. However, an examination of the physical market for gold indicates that the developing world (in the form of the E8, the eight largest developing economies) is responsible for far more physical gold demand than the G8 economies. For the white metals, a much greater percentage of demand is derived either directly or indi- rectly from G8 economic growth. To an extent, therefore, the comparative performance of these metals can be expected to reflect economic growth in the developed and developing world. During 2011, this was clearly the case with the most developed economies struggling to avoid recession but good levels of growth in China, India and elsewhere.

The platinum-gold spread Throughout the final months of 2011, we frequently discussed the platinum-gold spread and the fact that gold has traded at a premium to platinum since early September. This has happened before, in 1991 and 1993, but for only relatively short periods of time. For the majority of the period when platinum and gold prices have been freely set by market forces, platinum has traded at a premium to gold. In the minds of many people, including most of those working in the precious metals market, platinum is perceived as the premium metal and there is an expectation that platinum should have a higher price than gold. This expectation appears to be a simple psychological belief rather than one based on the utility of platinum or production costs or other more fundamentally- grounded arguments. However, it is clear that the reasons for buying and using the two metals are rather different.

Gold certainly benefits from perception as a “safe haven” against risk while platinum demand is driven higher by economic growth. 2011 has seen a rather unpleasant mix of fear over potential sovereign debt default and concern over a slowing global economy which helps explain gold’s outperformance of platinum. The premium of gold over platinum can thus be considered as a “fear gauge”. Our expectation is that investors will retain a rather depressed view of the world in early 2012 but will gradually realise that economic growth is likely to be a little stronger than consensus forecasts suggest. Although the premium for gold could increase over platinum initially, we expect it to narrow later in the year. It is possible that platinum will reach parity and even recover its traditional premium to gold even if gold appreciates in value. However, we believe, given the current gap in prices, that it is unlikely that average platinum prices will exceed average gold prices for the year.

Looking at the platinum market, however, the impact of this unexpected platinum discount is more complex. In the major developed world markets, platinum typically attracts a much-greater industry mark-up than gold anyway. So, a lower relative platinum price may provide an opportunity for retailers to maximise profits by selling more platinum instead of gold. Assuming that public perception still sees platinum as the premier metal (and this branding is extremely strong), platinum demand should increase in these markets. In China, the largest national market for platinum jewellery, platinum should also give the industry very strong profit potential. We expect competition between jewellers for this ex- tra profit to drive retail prices of platinum jewellery slowly lower, encouraging further de- mand. We have already seen the first signs of this with one jewellery chain reducing the price of platinum jewellery and seeing strong sales as a result.

chunk.text: 23 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Annual Precious Metal Report 17 January 2012 One final issue on the supply side, however, is the possibility of disruption to electricity supplies within South Africa. A few stories about the possibility of load-shedding or rolling black-outs in South Africa have surfaced already in 2012. Much power station mainte- nance is carried out in the Southern hemisphere summer and economic growth has also narrowed the spare generating capacity available to a very low level. Eskom has warned of the potential for disruption but, so far, there have been no signs of the problems which were seen leading up to the widespread blackouts of January 2008. While we acknowledge the potential for problems to occur, we note that the industry is rather better prepared on this occasion than it was then, with a greater degree of back-up power and more experience of operating with reduced electricity supply than previously. The Chamber of Mines commented that this is “merely the continuation of a long-existing situation”.

The European automotive market Given the economic troubles currently being experienced across Europe and the strong likelihood of a return to recession this year for many European countries, the outlook for automotive production is weak. Given the importance of the European diesel car sector to platinum demand, this is widely seen as a negative for platinum prices. The poor economic outlook has been confirmed by the decisions of a number of car makers to reduce their production forecasts for 2012. Investor demand As the palladium market showed last year, however, ETFs are a critical part of the physical metal supply and demand picture. Unfortunately, discerning the motivations of ETF investors is rather challenging. We feel, though, that two factors are key. Firstly, five years after the launch of the first physically-backed platinum ETFs, the market has become relatively mature. (Of course, the US-based ETF Securities fund and a num- ber of the European funds were launched in 2010 but the marketplace as a whole seems to be approaching maturity). If this is the case, then net inflows of metal can be expected to slow in the absence of other major stimuli. In a neutral year, we would expect to see some net inflows but these could be as low as 100,000 ounces, rather weaker than in the first few years of the ETFs’ existence as some consumers naturally sell “mature” invest- ments.

chunk.text: 26 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Annual Precious Metal Report 17 January 2012 Forecast 2012 High: $860, Average: $745, Low: $565 The palladium price confounded expectations in 2011 by falling in the second half of the year as investors sold their positions as the economic outlook worsened. In simple terms, speculators are now less exposed to this metal but remain firm believers in the underlying bullish fundamentals of strong automotive demand, limited scope for additional mine production, and the fact that Russian state stocks of this metal must, eventually, run out. We feel that these are all reasonable indicators of strong future palladium prices but it is not clear that most of the potential gains will be made during 2012. However, the outlook for the global automotive industry is positive (although much forward buying has already taken place) and economic growth should drive enhanced palladium demand from the electronics sector. There is little or no demand side price elasticity at current levels, so this market should move into a “fundamental” deficit if the Euro zone crisis does not worsen.

25 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Annual Precious Metal Report 17 January 2012 At the end of 2011, total net long speculative positions on NYMEX and TOCOM were 1.21 million ounces, over one million ounces lower than they were at their peak for the year in February. They are currently at their lowest level since September 2009 and while investor sentiment is negative, it should not be as negative as it was then. Noticeably, the number of long positions held has changed little but short interest has grown strongly. The market is certainly not over-exposed by historical standards yet sentiment (rightly or wrongly) remains almost uniformly bullish. Given this longer term bullishness, it seems likely that many of these short positions are held with a view to shorter term gains and that investor positions should lengthen again should prices show a determined move upwards, providing some support for the price. We feel that it is also important to note that other, non-conventional and hard-to-measure investment can have an impact upon this market.

chunk.text: 30 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Annual Precious Metal Report 17 January 2012 The rhodium market looks set to remain in a fundamental surplus during 2012. Forecast- ing supply of this metal is complicated for two reasons: at low prices, the primary produc- ers may not choose to sell all of the metal which they produce; and with platinum group metal basket prices (i.e. the price of the range of metals produced from mining) currently low, there is some prospect of the mining industry trimming its output, which would result in lower rhodium production. On balance, however, we expect rhodium production and sales to differ little from last year’s levels. On the demand side, the automotive market is key, having contributed 78 per cent of gross rhodium demand in 2011 according to Johnson Matthey’s latest review of the market. Despite the economic clouds gathering in Europe and slowing growth in some of the larger developing countries, the automotive market is expected to grow again in 2012, with rhodium demand rising as a result.

There may, though, be some buying to take advantage of low current prices by locking them in. The launch of a rhodium Exchange Traded Fund (ETF) in Q2 last year generated some excitement in this market and we still worry that it could introduce an unwelcome feedback mechanism between price formation and investor interest. With a full year of trading ahead, it seems reasonable to expect net buy- ing of up to 30,000 ounces this year. However, this would still leave the rhodium market in a fundamental surplus. This could lead to lower prices but we would expect a degree of bargain-hunting by consumers and a reluctance from the producers to sell all of their out- put at levels close to $1,000 per ounce, to provide some support to the price. Industrial demand for rhodium comes mainly from the glass and chemicals industries. While there will be some additions to new capacity, limited global growth means these will be relatively small. Overall, we therefore expect this market to remain in surplus. chunk.text: 31 Mitsui Global Precious Metals | Annual Precious Metal Report 17 January 2012 Looking at the ruthenium market, prices dropped substantially during 2011. There is little speculative or investor activity in ruthenium so price movements should normally reflect changes in the supply-demand balance. Having been stable at close to $180 for the first half of the year, the price started to decline in Q3, falling below $100 at the end of the year. Ruthenium demand fell last year as purchasing by the hard disk industry slowed. This did not represent lower usage of the metal but rather a decline in working stocks. We expect demand to improve next year, on the assumption that there will be no further stock reductions, with climbing hard disk output largely responsible. We believe, though, that the ruthenium market is currently in surplus with excess metal available and expect further downward pressure to be applied on the price throughout the first half of 2012 at least. The iridium market is rather less transparent than even the ruthenium market. We have seen indications of good demand for iridium crucibles for crystal growing and for iridium for other industrial applications.

constraints.condition: declined due to reduced purchasing by the hard disk industry

chunk.text: uthenium market. We have seen indications of good demand for iridium crucibles for crystal growing and for iridium for other industrial applications. However, it is not clear that this market is in a fundamental deficit that justifies higher prices. These higher prices may instead have been driven by similar bottlenecks and stockbuilding factors to those that drove ruthenium prices higher in 2006 and 2007. While we do not think it is intellectually reasonable to forecast prices in illiquid markets such as iridium, we are concerned about the longer term sustainability of current iridium prices on a demand basis. In our career of fourteen years in the precious metals industry, we have only been asked one question about the osmium market and we therefore do not cover it here.
